docs.title: SiriScore - Bitcoin Privacy Scorer
docs.text:
Score your Bitcoin transaction's privacy before you broadcast it. Paste a PSBT, raw tx hex, or txid and get a scored report with actionable findings before you sign.

Try SiriScore Install the library 82 Privacy Score Good

Verdicts: Good ≥ 70 · Fair 40–69 · Poor < 40

  • SiriScore web app: analyse a transaction right now
  • pip install siriscore: use as a Python library
  • CLI: btc-privacy-check --txid <txid>
  • REST API: integrate into your wallet or service

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
SiriScore runs up to 11 privacy heuristics against a transaction and produces a score from 0–100:

  • Penalty heuristics (H1–H8) deduct from the score when a privacy issue is detected
  • Positive heuristics (H9–H11) add to the score or suppress false positives when coinjoin or Payjoin patterns are detected

The score formula: min(100, max(0, 100 − sum(penalty weights) + sum(bonus weights)))

H8 (tainted UTXO) additionally caps the score at 40 regardless of other heuristics.

docs.title: Privacy by default
docs.text:
No transaction data leaves your machine unless you explicitly enable network checks (lookup=True). When network checks are on, only mempool.space is queried (with blockstream.info as fallback). You can point at your own Bitcoin node to avoid all third-party exposure entirely.

Privacy guarantee

SiriScore is a free, open-source Bitcoin privacy tool: a pre-broadcast transaction privacy analyzer that checks PSBTs, raw transactions, and txids for privacy leaks — address reuse, round amounts, coinjoin patterns — before chain analysis ever sees them. Try SiriScore, the Bitcoin transaction intelligence layer.

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
Fork the repo on GitHub, then clone your fork:

git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/siriscore.git
cd siriscore
git remote add upstream https://github.com/siri-score/siriscore.git
pip install -e ".[dev]"

Keep your fork in sync:

git fetch upstream
git rebase upstream/dev

docs.title: Branch naming
docs.text:
feature/issue-42/short-description
fix/issue-17/lookup-timeout
docs/update-heuristics-table

All PRs target the dev branch. main is release-only.

docs.title: Running tests
docs.text:
python3 -m pytest -q

Single file or test:

python3 -m pytest tests/test_heuristics.py -q
python3 -m pytest tests/test_heuristics.py::TestH2RoundAmount::test_fires_on_round_output -q

With coverage:

python3 -m pytest --cov=scorer -q

docs.title: Linting
docs.text:
ruff check scorer/ api/ tests/

Fix automatically:

ruff check --fix scorer/ api/ tests/

docs.title: PR process
docs.text:
1. Open a PR against dev (not main)
  2. CI must pass (tests + ruff + wheel build)
  3. One approval required
  4. Squash-merge preferred for small changes; merge commit for large features

docs.title: Adding a new heuristic
docs.text:
1. Create scorer/heuristics/hN_name.py with a single check function:
from scorer.report import Finding, Severity

def check(tx, psbt_meta) -> Finding | None:
    # tx has: .inputs, .outputs, .version, .locktime
    # psbt_meta is a dict of optional metadata
    if <condition>:
        return Finding(
            heuristic_id="HN",
            severity=Severity.WARNING,
            title="Short title",
            detail="What was found in this transaction.",
            suggestion="What the user should do about it.",
            weight=10,          # penalty weight; use negative for a bonus
            positive=False,     # True for privacy-positive findings (H9/H10/H11)
        )
    return None
  1. Add it to LOCAL (or NETWORK if it makes outbound calls) in scorer/heuristics/__init__.py:
from scorer.heuristics import hN_name
LOCAL = [..., hN_name]
  1. Add an entry to _HEURISTIC_DEFS in scorer/__init__.py:
("HN", Severity.WARNING, "Short title"),

  1. Add tests in tests/test_heuristics.py — at minimum one test that fires and one that does not.

  2. Update the heuristics overview table in the docs.

docs.title: Code owners
docs.text: Area Owner scorer/heuristics/ open scorer/lookup.py open api/ open web/ open CI / packaging @nkatha23

docs.title: REST API
docs.text: SiriScore ships a FastAPI backend that exposes the same scoring engine as the Python library over HTTP. Use it from any language, or wire it into a wallet or payment flow.

docs.title: Run the server
docs.text:
pip install siriscore
uvicorn api.main:app

The API is now at http://localhost:8000. The hosted instance behind siriscore.xyz exposes the same endpoints.

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
curl -s http://localhost:8000/score \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"input": "a4f1c9d2e3b5...", "input_type": "txid"}'

{
  "score": 72,
  "findings": [
    {
      "id": "H2",
      "title": "Round payment amount",
      "suggestion": "Consider using PayJoin to obscure the payment amount."
    }
  ],
  "checks": ["..."]
}

docs.title: Endpoints
docs.text: Endpoint Status Description POST /score Live Score a single transaction GET /labels / POST /labels Live Read and write the local label store. See Label System POST /labels/import Live Import a Sparrow BIP329 label export POST /score/batch Planned Score multiple transactions in one request GET /heuristics Planned Metadata for every heuristic;IDs, weights, severity GET /score/{txid} Planned Score by txid via a plain GET

docs.title: Privacy
docs.text: Calling a hosted instance sends your transaction data to that server, run the server locally to keep it on your machine. Scoring itself is offline by default: H3/H4 network lookups only run when the request sets "lookup": true.

docs.title: POST /score/batch
docs.text:
Status: Coming soon

Score multiple transactions in a single request.

{
  "transactions": [
    {"input": "cHNidP8BA...", "input_type": "psbt"},
    {"input": "a4f1c9d2...", "input_type": "txid"}
  ],
  "lookup": false
}

Returns an array of Report objects in the same order.

docs.title: GET /heuristics
docs.text:
Status: Coming soon

Returns metadata for all available heuristics: IDs, names, severity, weight, and whether a network connection is required.

[
  {
    "id": "H1",
    "name": "Script type mismatch",
    "severity": "critical",
    "weight": 25,
    "requires_network": false,
    "positive": false
  },
  ...
]

docs.title: GET /score/{txid}
docs.text:
Status: Coming soon

Score a transaction by txid via a GET request. Convenience endpoint for browser and webhook use.

GET /score/a4f1c9d2e3b5a6f7890abc1234567890abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567890ab

Equivalent to POST /score with input_type="txid" and lookup=true.

docs.title: POST /score
docs.text: Score a transaction via the REST API.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
POST /score
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "input": "cHNidP8BA...",
  "input_type": "psbt",
  "lookup": false
}
Field Type Default Description input string required PSBT (base64), raw tx hex, or txid input_type string "psbt" "psbt", "rawtx", or "txid" lookup boolean false Enable H3/H4 network checks

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "score": 72,
  "input_count": 2,
  "output_count": 2,
  "psbt_version": 0,
  "findings": [
    {
      "id": "H2",
      "severity": "warning",
      "title": "Round payment amount",
      "detail": "Output 0 has a round value of 10000000 sats (0.1 BTC).",
      "suggestion": "Consider using PayJoin to obscure the payment amount.",
      "weight": 15,
      "positive": false
    }
  ],
  "checks": [
    {"id": "H1", "status": "pass", "reason": "Script types are consistent."},
    {"id": "H2", "status": "fail", "reason": "Round output amount detected."},
    ...
  ],
  "labels": []
}

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8000/score \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"input": "cHNidP8BA...", "input_type": "psbt", "lookup": false}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool

docs.title: Error response
docs.text:
{"detail": "Could not parse input as PSBT, raw tx, or txid."}

HTTP 422 for parse errors.

docs.title: Why use SiriScore
docs.text: Every Bitcoin transaction is public forever. Before you broadcast one, SiriScore tells you what it reveals about you — so you can fix leaks while they can still be fixed.

docs.title: Why check before broadcasting
docs.text:
Once a transaction is on-chain it cannot be edited or recalled. Chain-analysis firms and curious observers apply well-known heuristics to cluster addresses, identify change outputs, and link transactions back to a single wallet. Most privacy leaks are cheap to avoid before signing — reorder outputs, avoid a round amount, skip a reused address — and impossible to undo afterwards.

SiriScore automates that pre-broadcast check: it runs the same style of heuristics an adversary would, and reports them to you first.

docs.title: What a transaction can leak
docs.text:
A few examples of what observers infer from a single transaction:

  • Which output is your change. If one output uses the same script type as the inputs and the other doesn't, or one amount is suspiciously round, the change output — and therefore your remaining balance — is exposed.
  • That your addresses belong to one wallet. Spending many inputs together, or paying to an address that has been used before, lets observers merge address clusters.
  • Which software built the transaction. Input/output ordering, locktime behaviour, and dust handling fingerprint specific wallets.

Each leak SiriScore detects maps to a documented heuristic with a weight and a suggested fix.

Full heuristics reference

docs.title: Who is it for
docs.text:
• Bitcoin users who want to understand whether a transaction they are about to broadcast leaks information about their wallet
  • Wallet developers who want to surface privacy warnings before the user signs
  • BTCPay Server operators who want to score transactions as part of a payment flow
  • Privacy researchers who want a programmatic interface to Bitcoin transaction analysis

docs.title: What it does not do
docs.text:
• It does not broadcast transactions
  • It does not store or transmit your transactions to any third party by default
  • It does not replace a full chain-analysis tool; it focuses on pre-broadcast, single-transaction analysis

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Use the web app: try it with no install
  • Install the library: Python API and CLI
  • Run locally: keep everything on your machine

docs.title: From PyPI
docs.text:
pip install siriscore

Requires Python 3.11 or later.

docs.title: From source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/siri-score/siriscore.git
cd siriscore
pip install -e ".[dev]"

The -e flag installs in editable mode — changes to scorer/, api/, or cli.py take effect immediately without reinstalling.

docs.title: Verify the install
docs.text:
import siriscore
print(siriscore.__version__)

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
The btc-privacy-check command and the siriscore alias are both installed automatically:

btc-privacy-check --txid a4f1c9d2e3b5a6f7890abc1234567890abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567890ab
siriscore --psbt cHNidP8BA...

docs.title: Common flags
docs.text: Flag Description --txid <txid> Score by transaction ID (fetches from mempool.space) --psbt <base64> Score a base64-encoded PSBT --rawtx <hex> Score a raw transaction hex --json Output machine-readable JSON --fail-below <N> Exit with code 1 if score is below N (CI use) --import-sparrow <file> Import Sparrow labels before scoring --lookup Enable network checks (H3, H4) --rpc-url <url> Bitcoin Core RPC endpoint --rpc-user <user> Bitcoin Core RPC username --rpc-password <pass> Bitcoin Core RPC password

docs.title: Point at your own node
docs.text:
Avoid sending txids to any third party by routing all lookups through your own Bitcoin Core node:

btc-privacy-check --txid <txid> \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8332 \
  --rpc-user alice \
  --rpc-password hunter2

Your node needs txindex=1 in bitcoin.conf.

docs.title: Exit codes
docs.text: Code Meaning 0 Scored at or above threshold 1 Score below --fail-below threshold 2 Parse error or network failure

docs.title: Run locally
docs.text: Running SiriScore locally means no transaction data is sent to siriscore.xyz. Your PSBT or txid stays on your machine.

docs.title: Start the server
docs.text:
pip install siriscore
uvicorn api.main:app --reload

Open http://localhost:8000.

To bind to a different port:

uvicorn api.main:app --reload --port 8080

docs.title: Offline demo
docs.text:
Open web/index.html directly in a browser — no server, no install:

# macOS
open web/index.html

# Linux
xdg-open web/index.html

# Windows
start web/index.html

The page detects it is running as a local file and falls back to built-in mock data automatically. Useful for UI exploration or demos.

docs.title: Development mode (from source)
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/siri-score/siriscore.git
cd siriscore
pip install -e ".[dev]"
uvicorn api.main:app --reload

Changes to scorer/, api/, or web/ are reflected immediately.

docs.title: Privacy when running locally
docs.text:
When the server is running on your machine, the only external calls are H3/H4 network lookups if you have Enable network checks toggled on. To disable those completely, turn off the toggle in the UI or pass lookup=False in the API request.

To route lookups through your own Bitcoin Core node instead of mempool.space:

btc-privacy-check --txid <txid> \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8332 \
  --rpc-user alice \
  --rpc-password hunter2

docs.title: Use the web app
docs.text: The fastest way to try SiriScore is the hosted tool at siriscore.xyz. No install required.

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Choose an input type using the tabs at the top of the form:

    • Txid — paste a 64-character transaction ID (most common)
    • PSBT — paste a base64-encoded partially signed Bitcoin transaction
    • Raw Tx — paste a raw transaction hex string

  2. Paste your transaction into the text field.

  3. Network checks — the toggle is on by default when the backend is running. This enables H3 (address reuse) and H4 (UTXO age clustering) by querying mempool.space. Turn it off if you do not want any data leaving your browser.

  4. Click Analyse Transaction.

docs.title: Reading the results
docs.text: Section What it shows Score gauge 0–100 with a colour band: red (Critical/Poor), orange (Fair), green (Good/Excellent) Findings Heuristics that fired, with detail and a suggested fix Checks Every heuristic with its status: pass / fail / skipped / unavailable Coin labels UTXO labels relevant to this transaction's inputs What to do next Prioritised action list

docs.title: Importing Sparrow labels
docs.text:
Click Import labels from Sparrow Wallet and select a .jsonl file exported from Sparrow Wallet (BIP329 format). Imported labels are stored locally in a SQLite database on the server and used to trigger H8 (tainted label detection).

Label system reference

docs.title: Running your own instance
docs.text:
If you want full privacy — no data to siriscore.xyz — run the server locally:

pip install siriscore
uvicorn api.main:app --reload
# open http://localhost:8000

Run locally

docs.title: Heuristics overview
docs.text: SiriScore runs 12 privacy checks. Penalty heuristics deduct from the score; positive heuristics add to it or suppress false positives.

docs.title: Penalty heuristics
docs.text: ID Name Severity Weight Requires network H1 Script type mismatch Critical 25 No H2 Round payment amount Warning 15 No H3 Address reuse on input Critical 20 Yes H4 UTXO age clustering Warning 10 Yes H5 High input count (CIOH) Warning 10 No H6 Dust input present Warning 10 No H7 Non-BIP69 ordering Info 5 No H8 Tainted labelled UTXO Critical 25 + score cap at 40 No H13 nLockTime anti-fee-sniping Info 5 No

docs.title: Positive heuristics
docs.text: ID Name Severity Effect What triggers it H9 Input is a coinjoin output Info Suppresses H5 Whirlpool denomination or coinjoin label H10 Transaction is a coinjoin Info +10 score bonus, suppresses H5 Whirlpool / Wasabi / JoinMarket structure H11 Payjoin opportunity Info / Warning No penalty BIP-21 URI with pj= parameter

docs.title: Silent payments (BIP-352)
docs.text: Silent payments awareness is built into H3. When H3 detects address reuse, it checks whether the recipient already supports BIP-352 silent payments and adjusts its suggestion accordingly.

docs.title: Scoring formula
docs.text:
score = min(100, max(0, 100 − sum(penalty weights) + sum(bonus weights)))
  • Weights are additive — multiple heuristics can fire on the same transaction
  • H10 uses weight = −10 (negative penalty = bonus)
  • H8 additionally caps the score at 40 regardless of other heuristics
  • H9 or H10 firing strips H5 from the findings (coinjoin breaks the CIOH assumption)

docs.title: H1 — Script type mismatch
docs.text: Severity: Critical · Weight: 25 · Network: No

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: All inputs use one script type but one or more outputs use a different type. For example: all inputs are P2WPKH (native SegWit) but one output is P2PKH (legacy).

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: A chain analyst can identify which output is the change by finding the one that matches the sender's script type. This reveals the payment direction and links the change output back to the sender's wallet.

docs.title: What to do
docs.text: Use the same script type for change outputs as your inputs. Most modern wallets do this automatically, but it is worth checking when constructing transactions manually or using coin control.

docs.title: False positive note
docs.text: If the recipient uses a different script type than you do, this finding may be unavoidable. In that case it is still worth noting — a privacy-conscious recipient would publish a same-type address.

docs.title: H10 — Coinjoin structure detection
docs.text: Severity: Info · Weight: −10 (bonus) · Network: No · Positive: Yes

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: The transaction being scored is itself a coinjoin, identified by one of three structural patterns:

docs.title: Whirlpool
docs.text: 3 or more outputs with equal values matching a Whirlpool denomination (100k / 1M / 5M / 50M sats) and at least 3 inputs.

docs.title: Wasabi / WabiSabi
docs.text: 5 or more equal-value outputs where the equal value is at least 10,000 sats (minimum viable coinjoin output).

docs.title: JoinMarket
docs.text: Equal number of inputs and outputs (minimum 3 each) — JoinMarket uses a symmetric structure.

docs.title: Effect on scoring
docs.text:
H10 is a positive finding:

  • +10 score bonus — the weight is −10, which subtracts from the penalty sum
  • H5 (CIOH) is suppressed — coinjoin transactions are expected to have many inputs from different wallets
  • Score is capped at 100

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: A coinjoin transaction is a deliberate privacy action. Penalising it with CIOH or other heuristics would be incorrect. H10 rewards the user for participating in a coinjoin.

docs.title: H11 — Payjoin opportunity
docs.text: Severity: Info (Warning if H5 also fires) · Weight: 0 · Network: No · Positive: Yes

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: The transaction's associated BIP-21 URI contains a pj= parameter, indicating the recipient supports BIP-77 async Payjoin.

docs.title: Effect on scoring
docs.text:
• No penalty — H11 is informational
  • Escalates to Warning when H5 (high input count / CIOH) also fires, because Payjoin directly addresses that risk
  • Appears green in the UI with a suggestion to use the Payjoin endpoint

docs.title: How to pass the BIP-21 URI
docs.text:
H11 reads payment_uri or bip21_uri from psbt_meta. Pass it via the library:

from siriscore import score_as

report = score_as(
    "cHNidP8BA...",
    input_type="psbt",
    psbt_meta={
        "payment_uri": "bitcoin:BC1Q...?amount=0.001&pj=https://btcpay.example.com/payjoin"
    },
)

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Payjoin (BIP-77) allows the recipient to contribute an input to the transaction, breaking the CIOH assumption and obscuring the payment amount. When a recipient supports it, the sender should use it — it improves privacy for both parties.

docs.title: Payjoin endpoint format
docs.text:
The pj= parameter contains an HTTPS URL — the sender posts the original PSBT there and receives a modified PSBT with the recipient's input added.

BTCPay Server integration

docs.title: Silent payments (BIP-352)
docs.text: Silent payments detection is not a standalone heuristic — it is built into H3 (address reuse).

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
When H3 detects address reuse on an input, it also checks whether any output pays to a BIP-352 silent payment address (identified by the sp1q / tsp1q prefix).

  • Recipient already supports silent payments — H3's suggestion reads: "Recipient supports silent payments. Future payments to this recipient will not reuse addresses."
  • Recipient does not support silent payments — H3 suggests requesting a silent payment address to eliminate reuse permanently.

docs.title: What is a silent payment address
docs.text:
BIP-352 silent payment addresses are static addresses that derive a unique one-time on-chain output per payment. The recipient scans the chain without publishing a fresh address per transaction.

Silent payment addresses start with sp1q (mainnet) or tsp1q (testnet/regtest).

docs.title: Scoring effect
docs.text: Silent payment awareness does not change H3's weight (20) or severity (Critical). It adjusts the suggestion text only.

docs.title: H13 — nLockTime anti-fee-sniping
docs.text: Severity: Info · Weight: 5 · Network: No

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: The transaction's nLockTime field is set to 0. Most well-behaved wallets (Bitcoin Core, Sparrow, etc.) set nLockTime to the current block height as an anti-fee-sniping measure.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text:
nLockTime = 0 is a wallet fingerprint. It reveals that the transaction was constructed by software that does not implement anti-fee-sniping — narrowing down which wallet or library was used. Blending in with the majority of transactions (which do set nLockTime) improves privacy.

Anti-fee-sniping protects against miners reorganising the chain to steal fees from recent blocks. Setting nLockTime to the current tip height makes the transaction invalid in any earlier block, removing the incentive to reorg.

docs.title: What to do
docs.text: Use a wallet that sets nLockTime to the current block height. Bitcoin Core, Sparrow Wallet, and most modern wallets do this by default.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text: Fires when tx.locktime == 0. Low-weight (5) informational finding — reduces the score slightly but is rarely the dominant issue.

docs.title: H2 — Round payment amount
docs.text: Severity: Warning · Weight: 15 · Network: No

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: One or more outputs have a round value in a common denomination — for example, exactly 0.01 BTC, 0.1 BTC, or 1 BTC (or their satoshi equivalents: 1,000,000 sat, 10,000,000 sat, etc.).

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Round amounts are a strong signal that an output is a payment rather than change. Change outputs are almost never round — they represent whatever is left after paying the recipient and fees. An analyst can identify the payment direction from this alone.

docs.title: What to do
docs.text:
• Use PayJoin (H11) to obscure the payment amount
  • Consider splitting the payment across multiple transactions
  • If using a wallet with coin control, avoid creating round change amounts

docs.title: H3 — Address reuse on input
docs.text: Severity: Critical · Weight: 20 · Network: Yes

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: One or more input addresses have been used in previous transactions (i.e. they have a transaction history on-chain).

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Bitcoin addresses are not meant to be reused. Each time you reuse an address, you link all transactions involving that address together — effectively creating a permanent public record connecting all your activity through that address.

docs.title: Network requirement
docs.text: H3 queries mempool.space (blockstream.info fallback) for the transaction history of each input address. It is skipped when lookup=False (the default).

docs.title: What to do
docs.text:
• Generate a fresh address for every transaction
  • Most modern HD wallets do this automatically — check your wallet settings
  • If you reused an address, consider consolidating those UTXOs in a coinjoin before spending further

docs.title: Privacy note
docs.text: Running H3 sends each input address to an external block explorer. Use --rpc-url to route lookups through your own node, or disable it with lookup=False if you do not want address data leaving your machine.

docs.title: H4 — UTXO age clustering
docs.text: Severity: Warning · Weight: 10 · Network: Yes

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: Two or more inputs were confirmed within 6 blocks of each other. This suggests they were received in the same time window — a signal that they likely came from the same wallet or activity cluster.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Block height is a proxy for time. Inputs confirmed close together are more likely to belong to the same wallet or to have been received in the same batch (e.g. an exchange withdrawal). This weakens the assumption that consolidating UTXOs from different sources hides their origin.

docs.title: Network requirement
docs.text: H4 fetches the block height of each input's prevout transaction from mempool.space. It is skipped when lookup=False.

docs.title: What to do
docs.text:
• Avoid consolidating UTXOs received in the same time window
  • Wait for more block confirmations between receive and spend
  • Use a wallet with coin control to select inputs from different time periods

docs.title: H5 — High input count (CIOH)
docs.text: Severity: Warning · Weight: 10 · Network: No

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: The transaction has 5 or more inputs. This triggers the Common Input Ownership Heuristic (CIOH): an analyst may assume all inputs are controlled by the same wallet.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Consolidating many inputs in a single transaction is a common pattern that reveals wallet structure. It effectively announces "these UTXOs all belong to the same entity."

docs.title: H9/H10 suppression
docs.text: When H9 or H10 fires (coinjoin input or coinjoin transaction detected), H5 is automatically suppressed. Coinjoin transactions intentionally have many inputs from different wallets — applying CIOH would be a false positive.

docs.title: What to do
docs.text:
• Limit consolidation to 2–4 inputs per transaction
  • Consider using a coinjoin to merge UTXOs without revealing common ownership
  • If you must consolidate many inputs, do it in a dedicated consolidation transaction when fees are low, rather than combining it with a payment

docs.title: H6 — Dust input present
docs.text: Severity: Warning · Weight: 10 · Network: No

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: One or more inputs have a value below the dust threshold (typically 546 satoshis for P2PKH, 294 sat for P2WPKH).

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Dust inputs are often the result of a dust attack — an adversary sends a tiny amount to an address to link it to other UTXOs when the recipient spends the dust. Spending dust confirms to the attacker that you control the address.

docs.title: What to do
docs.text:
• Do not spend dust UTXOs unless you are prepared to link your wallet cluster
  • Most wallets allow you to freeze or ignore dust inputs via coin control
  • Consider labelling dust UTXOs and excluding them from future transactions

docs.title: H7 — Non-BIP69 ordering
docs.text: Severity: Info · Weight: 5 · Network: No

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: The inputs or outputs are not sorted according to BIP69 (lexicographic ordering of inputs by txid/vout, outputs by value/script).

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Non-deterministic ordering leaks information about which output is change. The change output is often appended last by wallets that do not randomise output ordering. BIP69 standardises the order so that ordering alone reveals nothing.

docs.title: What to do
docs.text: Use a wallet that implements BIP69 output ordering, or one that randomises output order. Most modern wallets do one or the other.

docs.title: Note
docs.text: This is an informational finding with a low weight (5). It is unlikely to be the deciding factor in a score but is worth addressing alongside other findings.

docs.title: H8 — Tainted labelled UTXO
docs.text: Severity: Critical · Weight: 25 · Network: No · Score cap: 40

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text: One or more inputs match a UTXO, address, or transaction that has been imported into the local label store with a tainted tag.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: A tainted UTXO is one that is known to be linked to a problematic source — for example, a direct withdrawal from a KYC exchange, funds received from a flagged address, or a coin explicitly marked as high-risk. Spending a tainted UTXO directly links your transaction to that source.

docs.title: Score cap
docs.text: When H8 fires, the final score is additionally capped at 40, regardless of how well the transaction performs on other heuristics. This reflects the severity of spending a known-tainted coin.

docs.title: Label matching
docs.text:
H8 checks three match types against the label store:

  1. UTXO match — input's txid:vout matches a stored output or input label
  2. Address match — input's address matches a stored addr label
  3. Transaction match — input's prevout txid matches a stored tx label

docs.title: What to do
docs.text:
• Avoid spending tainted UTXOs directly into regular transactions
  • Consider using a coinjoin to break the link before spending further
  • Review your label store — a tainted tag on a transaction labels all its outputs, which may be broader than intended

Label system Importing from Sparrow

docs.title: H9 — Coinjoin input detection
docs.text: Severity: Info · Weight: 0 · Network: No · Positive: Yes

docs.title: What it detects
docs.text:
One or more inputs appear to come from a previous coinjoin transaction, identified by:

  • Input value matches a Whirlpool denomination: 100,000 / 1,000,000 / 5,000,000 / 50,000,000 satoshis
  • Input UTXO is labelled coinjoin in the local label store

docs.title: Effect on scoring
docs.text:
H9 is a positive finding — it adds no penalty. When H9 fires:

  • H5 (CIOH) is suppressed — a coinjoin-sourced input breaks the common-input-ownership assumption, so penalising for CIOH would be a false positive
  • The finding appears green in the UI

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Spending a coinjoin output is a privacy-positive action — the UTXO's history is broken from its pre-coinjoin chain. Recognising this prevents the scorer from incorrectly penalising coinjoin users.

docs.title: Label System
docs.text: SiriScore stores coin labels locally in SQLite. Labels drive H8 (tainted UTXO) and H9 (coinjoin input) detection.

docs.title: Label format
docs.text: SiriScore stores coin labels in a local SQLite database at ~/.utxo-privacy-scorer/labels.db. No cloud sync, no telemetry.

docs.title: BIP329 JSONL (Sparrow export)
docs.text:
The primary import format is BIP329 JSONL — one JSON object per line:

{"type":"tx","ref":"<txid>","label":"Exchange withdrawal","tag":"tainted"}
{"type":"addr","ref":"bc1q...","label":"KYC receive address","tag":"tainted"}
{"type":"output","ref":"<txid>:0","label":"Coinjoin output","tag":"coinjoin"}
{"type":"input","ref":"<txid>:0","label":"Spent coinjoin input","tag":"coinjoin"}

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Field Description type tx, addr, output, or input ref The identifier — txid for tx, address for addr, txid:vout for output/input label Human-readable description tag Optional scoring tag (see below)

docs.title: Tag types
docs.text: Tag types reference

docs.title: Legacy format
docs.text:
The older simple JSON shape {"txid:vout": "label"} is also accepted for backward compatibility:

{
  "35cebb10:1": "tainted",
  "bc1qalum...": "KYC address"
}

docs.title: How H8 uses labels
docs.text: H8 fires when any input in the analysed transaction matches a label record with tag: tainted.

docs.title: Match logic
docs.text:
For each input, H8 checks three match types in order:

  1. UTXO match — txid:vout of the input matches a stored output or input label
  2. Address match — the input's spending address matches a stored addr label
  3. Transaction match — the input's prevout txid matches a stored tx label

If any input matches on any of these three, H8 fires.

docs.title: Score effect
docs.text:
When H8 fires:

  • Penalty weight of 25 is added to the score calculation
  • The final score is additionally capped at 40 regardless of all other heuristics

This cap reflects the severity of unknowingly spending a known-tainted coin — even a transaction that passes every other check is considered high-risk if it spends a tainted UTXO.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
You import this label:

{"type":"tx","ref":"93c98c2a...","label":"Binance withdrawal","tag":"tainted"}

Later you analyse a transaction that spends an output from 93c98c2a.... H8 fires because the input's prevout txid matches the tx label — even though you labelled the transaction, not the specific output.

docs.title: Avoiding false positives
docs.text: Be precise with tx labels — they match all outputs of a transaction. If only one specific UTXO from a transaction is tainted, use an output label (txid:vout) instead.

docs.title: Export from Sparrow Wallet
docs.text:
1. Open Sparrow Wallet
  2. Go to File → Export Wallet
  3. Select Labels — this exports a .jsonl file in BIP329 format

docs.title: Import into SiriScore
docs.text:
Web UICLILibraryREST API

Click Import labels from Sparrow Wallet on the SiriScore home page and select your .jsonl file.

btc-privacy-check --psbt <b64> --import-sparrow sparrow-labels.jsonl

import siriscore
n = siriscore.import_labels("sparrow-labels.jsonl")
print(f"Imported {n} labels")

curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/labels/import \
  -F "file=@sparrow-labels.jsonl"

docs.title: What gets imported
docs.text:
SiriScore processes three BIP329 record types:

Type ref format Effect tx txid Labels all outputs of that transaction addr bitcoin address Labels UTXOs received to that address output txid:vout Labels a specific UTXO

Labels with tag: tainted trigger H8 when a matching input is found. Labels with tag: coinjoin trigger H9.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Create a test file sparrow-labels.jsonl:

{"type":"tx","ref":"93c98c2a742373460e74b7d3b39ba30283b14476df835916d0a3f60dfc988e0d","label":"Exchange withdrawal","tag":"tainted"}
{"type":"addr","ref":"bc1qalum72s7tmt39y32fv5r06qvu9nz5phdjfcw8h","label":"KYC receive address","tag":"tainted"}
{"type":"output","ref":"35cebb10f6d6129716effcd69eb43df40669f4b27533be0857299fec0c52a976:1","label":"Labelled coin","tag":"tainted"}

Then analyse txid 93c98c2a... — H8 should fire because the transaction itself is tainted.

docs.title: Tag types
docs.text:
The tag field on a label record controls how SiriScore uses it during scoring.

Tag Effect tainted Triggers H8 — score is penalised (weight 25) and capped at 40 coinjoin Triggers H9 — positive finding, suppresses H5 (CIOH) clean Informational only — no scoring effect unknown Informational only — no scoring effect

docs.title: tainted
docs.text:
Use tainted for UTXOs you know are linked to a problematic source:

  • Direct withdrawal from a KYC exchange
  • Received from a flagged or identified address
  • Explicitly marked as high-risk by your threat model

Spending a tainted UTXO will always result in H8 firing and a score cap of 40.

docs.title: coinjoin
docs.text:
Use coinjoin for UTXOs that came from a coinjoin transaction when the value alone does not match a Whirlpool denomination (e.g. a JoinMarket or Wasabi output at a non-standard amount).

When an input has the coinjoin tag, H9 fires as a positive finding and H5 (CIOH) is suppressed.

docs.title: clean / unknown
docs.text: These are for your own bookkeeping. They do not affect the score but appear in the Coin labels section of the report.

docs.title: Setting tags in Sparrow
docs.text:
In Sparrow Wallet, the label export (File → Export Wallet → Labels) does not always include explicit tags. You can add them manually in the exported JSONL, or use SiriScore's REST API to add labels with tags directly:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/labels \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"ref": "txid:vout", "type": "output", "label": "Exchange withdrawal", "tag": "tainted"}'

docs.title: Library Reference
docs.text:
The siriscore Python library scores a transaction and returns a structured report you can render, log, or gate on. Install it with pip install siriscore.

See Install the library for requirements and CLI setup.

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
import siriscore

# Accepts a PSBT (base64), raw tx hex, or txid — type is auto-detected
report = siriscore.score("cHNidP8BA...")

print(report.score)  # 82

for finding in report.findings:
    print(f"{finding.heuristic_id}: {finding.title}")
    print(f"  → {finding.suggestion}")

docs.title: Choosing a function
docs.text: You want to Use Score a transaction; let the input type be auto-detected score() Skip auto-detection, or pass psbt_meta for Payjoin detection (H11) score_as() Import wallet labels so label-based scoring (H8/H9) has data to match import_labels()

docs.title: Understanding results
docs.text:
Every call returns a Report, ie the score, the Finding entries for heuristics that fired, and a checks list recording what every heuristic did, including the ones that passed or were skipped.

  • Report and Finding models - every field, with the score formula
  • Check statuses - pass / fail / skipped / unavailable and when each occurs

docs.title: Privacy
docs.text:
Scoring runs entirely offline unless you opt in to network lookups with lookup=True, and you can route those through your own node.

Privacy guarantee

docs.title: Check statuses
docs.text:
Every heuristic produces a Check entry in report.checks, even when it does not fire. This gives a complete audit trail of what ran, what passed, and what was skipped.

Status Meaning pass Heuristic ran and found no issue. Also used for suppressed H5 (when H9/H10 fires) and for positive findings (H9/H10/H11 when they fire). fail Heuristic fired as a penalty finding — appears in report.findings. skipped Network check deliberately not run (lookup=False). Applies to H3 and H4. unavailable Could not run — required input data was missing. Common when a PSBT does not include prevout values.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
for check in report.checks:
    print(f"{check.heuristic_id:4} {check.status:12} {check.reason}")

Typical output for an offline score:

H1   pass         Script types are consistent across inputs and outputs
H2   pass         No round output amounts detected
H3   skipped      Network lookups disabled
H4   skipped      Network lookups disabled
H5   pass         Input count (2) is below the consolidation threshold
H6   pass         No dust inputs detected
H7   pass         Inputs and outputs follow BIP69 ordering
H8   pass         No tainted labels matched
H9   pass         No coinjoin inputs detected
H10  pass         Transaction does not match any coinjoin structure
H11  pass         No BIP-21 URI with pj= parameter found

docs.title: import_labels()
docs.text:
siriscore.import_labels(path: str, *, source: str = "sparrow") -> int

Imports coin labels from a file into the local SQLite label store (~/.utxo-privacy-scorer/labels.db). Returns the number of labels imported.

Labels are used by H8. If a tainted UTXO is found among the transaction's inputs, H8 fires and the score is capped at 40.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description path str required Path to the labels file source str "sparrow" Import format — currently only "sparrow" is supported

docs.title: Supported format
docs.text:
Sparrow Wallet BIP329 JSONL export, one JSON object per line:

{"type":"tx","ref":"93c98c2a...","label":"Exchange withdrawal","tag":"tainted"}
{"type":"addr","ref":"bc1qalum...","label":"KYC address","tag":"tainted"}
{"type":"output","ref":"35cebb...:1","label":"Labelled coin","tag":"coinjoin"}

Field tag drives scoring: tainted triggers H8; coinjoin triggers H9 (suppresses H5).

Tag types reference

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import siriscore

n = siriscore.import_labels("sparrow-labels.jsonl")
print(f"Imported {n} labels")

# Labels are now used automatically when scoring
report = siriscore.score("cHNidP8BA...")

docs.title: Report
docs.text:
@dataclass
class Report:
    score: int               # 0–100
    findings: list[Finding]  # only heuristics that fired
    checks: list[Check]      # every heuristic, with its status
    labels: list[dict]       # UTXO labels relevant to this transaction's inputs
    input_count: int
    output_count: int
    psbt_version: int | None # None for raw tx / txid inputs

score is computed as min(100, max(0, 100 − sum(weights))). Negative weights (H10: −10) add to the score. When H8 fires, the score is additionally capped at 40.

docs.title: Finding
docs.text:
@dataclass
class Finding:
    heuristic_id: str    # "H1"–"H11"
    severity: Severity
    title: str
    detail: str          # what was observed in this transaction
    suggestion: str      # what to do about it
    weight: int          # penalty added to score calculation
    positive: bool       # True for H9/H10/H11 (rendered green in the UI)

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
@dataclass
class Check:
    heuristic_id: str
    status: str          # "pass" | "fail" | "skipped" | "unavailable"
    reason: str          # human-readable explanation

Check statuses

docs.title: Severity
docs.text:
class Severity(str, Enum):
    CRITICAL = "critical"
    WARNING  = "warning"
    INFO     = "info"

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import siriscore

report = siriscore.score("cHNidP8BA...")

print(f"Score: {report.score}/100")
print(f"Inputs: {report.input_count}, Outputs: {report.output_count}")

for f in report.findings:
    flag = "✓" if f.positive else "✗"
    print(f"{flag} [{f.heuristic_id}] {f.severity.value.upper()}: {f.title}")
    print(f"   {f.detail}")
    print(f"   Fix: {f.suggestion}")

for c in report.checks:
    print(f"{c.heuristic_id}: {c.status}")

docs.title: Privacy guarantee
docs.text: SiriScore is designed so that no transaction data leaves your machine by default.

docs.title: What makes network calls
docs.text:
Heuristic What is sent Destination H3: Address reuse Each input address mempool.space (blockstream.info fallback) H4: UTXO age clustering Each input's prevout txid mempool.space (blockstream.info fallback)

Network checks are opt-out by default. They only run when you explicitly pass lookup=True (library / CLI) or toggle on "Enable network checks" (web UI).

All other heuristics (H1, H2, H5, H6, H7, H8, H9, H10, H11) run entirely offline against the transaction data you provide.

docs.title: Point at your own node
docs.text:
To run H3 and H4 without sending anything to a third party, configure your own Bitcoin Core node:

# bitcoin.conf
txindex=1

# CLI
btc-privacy-check --txid <txid> \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8332 \
  --rpc-user alice \
  --rpc-password hunter2

# Library
report = siriscore.score(
    txid,
    rpc_url="http://127.0.0.1:8332",
    rpc_user="alice",
    rpc_password="hunter2",
)

When rpc_url is set, all network lookups go to your node. No data reaches mempool.space or blockstream.info.

Note

H3 (address reuse) is automatically skipped when using RPC mode because Bitcoin Core's RPC does not expose a full address transaction history index by default. H4 (UTXO age) still runs via gettxout / block height lookups.

docs.title: Label store
docs.text: Labels are stored in ~/.utxo-privacy-scorer/labels.db, a local SQLite file. No cloud sync, no telemetry.

docs.title: The web app at siriscore.xyz
docs.text:
When you use the hosted tool at siriscore.xyz, your PSBT or txid is sent to the siriscore.xyz server over HTTPS. For maximum privacy, run the server locally instead.

Run locally

docs.title: score_as()
docs.text:
siriscore.score_as(input_str: str, *, input_type: str, lookup: bool = False, psbt_meta: dict | None = None, **kwargs) -> Report

Like score() but with an explicit input type. Use this when you know the input format and want to skip auto-detection, or when you need to pass psbt_meta for H11 (Payjoin detection).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description input_str str required PSBT, raw tx hex, or txid input_type str required "psbt", "rawtx", or "txid" lookup bool False Enable H3/H4 network lookups psbt_meta dict None Extra metadata — payment_uri or bip21_uri for H11

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
from siriscore import score_as

# Explicit PSBT
report = score_as("cHNidP8BA...", input_type="psbt")

# Raw tx
report = score_as("0200000001...", input_type="rawtx")

# Txid with network checks
report = score_as("a4f1c9d2...", input_type="txid", lookup=True)

# With Payjoin endpoint for H11
report = score_as(
    "cHNidP8BA...",
    input_type="psbt",
    psbt_meta={
        "payment_uri": "bitcoin:BC1Q...?amount=0.001&pj=https://btcpay.example.com/payjoin"
    },
)

docs.title: score()
docs.text:
siriscore.score(input_str: str, *, lookup: bool = False, rpc_url: str | None = None, rpc_user: str | None = None, rpc_password: str | None = None) -> Report

Accepts a PSBT (base64), raw transaction hex, or txid. Returns a Report.

The input type is auto-detected: - 64-character hex string → txid - Non-64 hex string → raw transaction - Everything else → PSBT (base64)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description input_str str required PSBT (base64), raw tx hex, or txid lookup bool False Enable H3/H4 network lookups via mempool.space rpc_url str None Bitcoin Core RPC endpoint — routes all lookups through your node rpc_user str None Bitcoin Core RPC username rpc_password str None Bitcoin Core RPC password

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
import siriscore

# Fully offline — H3 and H4 skipped
report = siriscore.score("cHNidP8BA...")

# With network checks (mempool.space / blockstream.info)
report = siriscore.score("cHNidP8BA...", lookup=True)

# Via your own Bitcoin Core node
report = siriscore.score(
    "a4f1c9d2e3b5...",
    rpc_url="http://127.0.0.1:8332",
    rpc_user="alice",
    rpc_password="hunter2",
)

# Score by txid
report = siriscore.score("a4f1c9d2e3b5...", lookup=True)

print(report.score)           # 72
print(report.input_count)     # 2
print(report.output_count)    # 2

docs.title: Return value
docs.text: Report

docs.title: Wallet Integration Guide
docs.text: There are two alternative implementations in this section: use SiriScore alongside a wallet you already run, or build it into a wallet you're developing.

docs.title: Use SiriScore with your wallet
docs.text: Wallet Status What you get Sparrow Wallet Live Score PSBTs before signing; export BIP329 labels so H8/H9 can match your coins BTCPay Server Planned Score payments with Payjoin detection (H11) in the invoice flow Cake Wallet Planned In-wallet scoring via the library / a Dart FFI binding

docs.title: Build SiriScore into your wallet
docs.text:
Any wallet can add pre-broadcast privacy scoring. The pieces:

1. Pick an integration surface. The Python library gives you the richest interface (Report objects, label store access); the REST API works from any language; the CLI suits shell-based flows. All three patterns, with code, are in the generic integration guide.

2. Apply the core pattern. Score the transaction after construction but before signing, show the findings and their suggestions to the user, and warn or block below a score threshold. The BTCPay guide shows a working example of threshold gating.

3. Make it a complete integration. The difference between a basic and a thorough integration is three touchpoints:

  • Pass the payment URI. Supply the BIP-21 URI as psbt_meta={"payment_uri": ...} via score_as() so Payjoin opportunities (H11) are detected
  • Import the wallet's labels. Feed BIP329 labels to import_labels() so tainted-coin (H8) and coinjoin (H9) detection have data to match
  • Respect the privacy defaults. Keep lookup=False unless the user opts in, and let them point network checks at their own node. See the privacy guarantee

Building an integration for a wallet not listed here? We'd like to hear about it.

Contributing

docs.title: BTCPay Server — Payjoin + scoring
docs.text: BTCPay Server supports BIP-77 async Payjoin out of the box. When a customer pays a BTCPay invoice with a Payjoin-capable wallet, the transaction has a pj= parameter in the BIP-21 URI — which SiriScore detects as H11.

docs.title: Scoring a BTCPay payment
docs.text:
When a BTCPay invoice is paid, you can score the PSBT before broadcasting by passing the full BIP-21 URI:

from siriscore import score_as

# BIP-21 URI from BTCPay invoice
payment_uri = "bitcoin:BC1QXXX...?amount=0.01&pj=https://btcpay.example.com/BTC/pj"

report = score_as(
    psbt_base64,
    input_type="psbt",
    psbt_meta={"payment_uri": payment_uri},
)

if report.score < 60:
    print("Warning: low privacy score before broadcast")

docs.title: H11 and Payjoin
docs.text:
When the pj= endpoint is present, H11 fires as a positive (green) finding:

  • Info — Payjoin endpoint available, consider using it
  • Warning — escalates when H5 (high input count / CIOH) also fires, because Payjoin directly mitigates that risk

docs.title: Webhook integration (coming)
docs.text: A future version of SiriScore will support webhook callbacks so BTCPay can automatically score transactions and flag low-scoring payments for review.

docs.title: Cake Wallet — SDK integration
docs.text:
Status: Coming soon

Cake Wallet integration via the SiriScore Python library (or a planned Dart/FFI binding) will allow mobile users to score transactions before broadcasting directly within the wallet UI.

docs.title: Library (Python)
docs.text:
Any wallet with a Python layer can integrate SiriScore directly:

import siriscore

def pre_broadcast_check(psbt_base64: str) -> dict:
    report = siriscore.score(psbt_base64)
    return {
        "score": report.score,
        "findings": [
            {"id": f.heuristic_id, "title": f.title, "suggestion": f.suggestion}
            for f in report.findings if not f.positive
        ],
    }

docs.title: REST API
docs.text:
Any wallet with HTTP support can POST to the /score endpoint:

POST https://your-siriscore-instance.example.com/score
Content-Type: application/json

{"input": "<psbt-or-txid>", "input_type": "psbt", "lookup": false}

For maximum user privacy, run a local SiriScore instance and point the wallet at http://127.0.0.1:8000.

docs.title: CLI (shell wallets / scripts)
docs.text:
RESULT=$(btc-privacy-check --psbt "$PSBT" --json)
SCORE=$(echo "$RESULT" | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['score'])")
if [ "$SCORE" -lt 60 ]; then
  echo "Low privacy score: $SCORE"
fi

docs.title: Sparrow Wallet — PSBT export flow
docs.text: Sparrow Wallet can export PSBTs and BIP329 labels that work directly with SiriScore.

docs.title: Analyse a transaction before signing
docs.text:
1. In Sparrow, go to Send and construct your transaction
  2. Click Create Transaction then Export PSBT
  3. Save the .psbt file or copy the base64 string
  4. Paste into SiriScore — either at siriscore.xyz or via CLI:
btc-privacy-check --psbt "$(cat transaction.psbt | base64 -w0)"

The report will show you which privacy issues exist before you sign.

docs.title: Export coin labels
docs.text:
Sparrow's label export (BIP329 JSONL) lets SiriScore detect tainted inputs (H8) and coinjoin outputs (H9).

  1. In Sparrow, go to File → Export Wallet
  2. Select Labels and export as .jsonl
  3. Import into SiriScore:
# CLI
btc-privacy-check --psbt <b64> --import-sparrow sparrow-labels.jsonl

# Library
import siriscore
siriscore.import_labels("sparrow-labels.jsonl")

# Web UI — click "Import labels from Sparrow Wallet"

docs.title: Label format
docs.text:
Sparrow exports one JSON object per line:

{"type":"tx","ref":"<txid>","label":"Exchange withdrawal","tag":"tainted"}
{"type":"addr","ref":"bc1q...","label":"KYC address","tag":"tainted"}
{"type":"output","ref":"<txid>:0","label":"Coinjoin output","tag":"coinjoin"}

Label system reference

docs.title: Supported Sparrow versions
docs.text: Sparrow v1.8+ using the BIP329 JSONL export format. The older simple {"txid:vout": "label"} JSON shape is also supported for backward compatibility.
